turns.content: Hi, I'm new to Rust and want to port an existing project. How can I use mutation testing during my porting process?

Great question! Mutation testing is a powerful technique that helps ensure your tests are robust by introducing subtle bugs (mutations) into the codebase and seeing if your test suite catches them. This can be especially useful when you're porting existing projects, as it helps verify that new tests cover edge cases.
What tools do I need to set up mutation testing in Rust?

For mutation testing in Rust on the nightly channel, you can use the `mutagen` crate. It's an excellent choice because it supports LLVM instrumentation and mutation operators tailored for Rust. First, you'll need to run your tests with `cargo test`, then use `mutagen` to generate mutants and analyze them.
Can I explain a bit more about how mutation testing works?

Sure! Mutation testing involves running your test suite against a modified version of your codebase with small changes (mutations). If at least one mutant causes the tests to fail, that means your original tests are effective. Otherwise, if all mutants survive, it suggests your tests aren't comprehensive enough. It’s like fuzzing but for your tests—it helps you identify fragile or incomplete test cases.
Do I need to instrument my code myself?

No, `mutagen` handles instrumentation automatically. All you need is to run your tests with the mutagen tool and it will modify your binary in memory during execution. Just make sure you're on the nightly Rust channel because mutation testing requires LLVM features available there.
